sentences.text: It was one bomb.
Then, of course, everybody started to get afraid and to see what we're going to do.
My parents and other people of that age, which they were in their late 40s, early or late 40s, remembered the First World War.
And they said that when Germans come in, the first thing, they grabbed men to do work.
So what is there to do?
We have to run away.
Being that we had some trucks, my father and my brother and I and some other friends also, we loaded up a truck and we went east, as far as the Russian border, to a city called Mezhirich.
It's on the southeast border between Poland and Russia.
We did not hear what had happened to Kaluszyn and that town.
We left about the 7th of July-- of, excuse me-- September.
--about September 7th.
And we were there until about 10 days until the Russian army came in to Poland and they occupied.
Being that they occupied, we says, no, right now, we're going to go with the Russians back to Kaluszyn.

As we were traveling, not with them, but after that, their military went first, we heard that the Russian supposedly and the Germans had made an agreement that the Russians will be on one side of the Vistula, of the river that divides Warsaw.
One side is Praga and one side is Warsaw.
And the east side will be for Russians, and the west side will be for the Germans.
So we were glad that this is going to be like that.
So our town will be under Russian occupation.
And when we came to a town called Siedlce, which is 33 kilometers from Warsaw, we noticed that the Russian army is coming back.
They're not going towards Warsaw, but they're coming from Warsaw.
And we're told that the agreement supposedly made is not the agreement, that they going up to Brest, Brest-Litovsk.
So being that we were so close, and being that we were about 33 kilometers from our town, we heard what happened to Kaluszyn, that the town was destroyed.
The center of town was all destroyed.
People ran away.
There was no place to live, this and that.
So we left in our home, my mother, my sister, and my grandmother, my grandma-- my father's mother-- in the house.
So we wanted to go to out to see what happened to them.
When we came to our town, we couldn't get into our house, because strange people were living there.
Because everything was destroyed, they had to find some places.
Somehow we got in there, and we had to get the people out.
So we hired horses and buggies, horses and wagons, to take the people to other places.
Some had relatives in another town, and so we paid-- we paid them to go out, and we paid their transportation to leave the house back to us.
And we took in some relatives that were burned out.
And this is how we remained in Kaluszyn.
My mother, they were in-- they took all the-- most of the Jews took into church.

The church was a large church in there, because they had to keep them-- to save the population, the Germans want to save the population and fight only the military, because they took-- and being in the church, they were all afraid that this is what's going to happen.
They're going to bomb the church and everybody in it.
But this didn't happen, thank God.
And they survived.
We remained under German rule.
That was in 1939, the end of September when we came back.
Much more difficult became when they started to prepare for against-- well, actually I cannot describe when it came, because it was always difficult.
As we say it in Yiddish, yesterday was a good day, because every day that was, was a bad day.
Yesterday was a good day.
When my father was selected for one of the Judenrat, there was a lot of people, wealthy people and known people, good senior citizens of the town, known all over, and my father was known all over.
And they start to see-- start to discuss, how can we break the German will?
They should not punish us so much.
The only way to do it is buy them off.
So they decided, each one of Judenrat had a mission, and whether my father volunteered to it or was appointed, that they should be going to the Germans and negotiate with them.
But this was thought about.
But before this happened, the Germans themselves, when they came over to town, our house was the first house-- Jewish house-- and they came in right away there.
And they saw this, we want this, we want that.
They started to beat and this and that.
And came to a position that my father got friendly with them-- friendly with them, and they said, you need something, you have a wife, you need a ring, and started to buy them off.
And it started, whatever it came a law, where the Jews to go here or not to go there, used to be flatten it down.
Instead of 100%, used to be 80% and less and less.
And this is how we lived.

Not only us, but there was the county seat, which is Minsk Mazowiecki, which the main governor of the county was there, was all the Jews, and they realized-- they started first, and they told us-- by us, I mean they told the Judenrat in Kaluszyn, we got to work together.
We got to help each other.
This is how we're doing it.
So we got to buy them in so that they should know that by having something, they've got to give something.
And this is what happened.
And when whenever they came, it was always-- and being friendly to my father, to the Jews, the Poles also became a little softer.
They said, like the Germans working with the Jews.
And there were many times, they used to requisite-- they used to take away, you know, the people used to have merchandise, let's say, leather or material they used to bring in from Warsaw.
And they  used to come in, they used to take it away.
Because you're not allowed to sell.
Where did you get it?
So by somehow we used to take it back.
They used to take it away and my father used to go there, bring it back.
They were also taking away-- the farmers used to take their wares to Warsaw, like potatoes or rye or wheat or anything.
And they used to pass by the place, and they used to take it away from the farmers.
Being that we had so many people in our town, so many hungry and poor and so on, we used to ask the Germans to give us a wagon of potatoes.
And every once in awhile, a week or two weeks used to bring in to feed because-- to feed the poor, because they made a kitchen for the poor.
A soup kitchen.
My mother was working in the kitchen with other people.
And this is how we got them in.
So when it came to make the ghetto, it was made the ghetto wherever you lived.
If a Jew lived, let's say 300 yards among the Poles, there was a ghetto also.
He lives there, it's a ghetto.
So that helped a lot.

And compared to other towns and places where they had maybe more stronger or less able-- less able men to work with the Germans to convince them that, after all, you are a father, you are a son, how can you stand, how can you do that?
We did not have that better time.
Our house was in the ghetto.
Oh, there was a lot of houses around near us and around the city.
There was no fence.
There was no fence, but each one knew where the ghetto is.
But because people lived outside the ghetto and it was called ghetto, so there was no problem.
Then there were time a lot of people from town lived from the village.
They used to go to the farmers in the little village and used to bring in pair of shoes, piece of leather, or a piece of-- a pair of pants, used to sell their own things.
Let's say they had bedding.
They had bedding for four or five people in the house, and they didn't have a bed to sleep.
They use to sleep two or three in one, and they used to use it instead of each one had a cover, they used to cover themselves two or three cover with the same thing.
They used to sell those.
And those used to go in the village, there came a law not to allow them, not to go to the village.
Only those that have a permit, those can go.
So other people started to come to Judenrat, I need to live.
So my father went to the Germans, look, I have people that live in the village this, this.
Whatever shoes they gave them, they used to bring permits.
So how many permits can you bring?
How many permits can you get?
Everybody wanted but you couldn't.
You couldn't satisfy everyone.
But he did the most he could.
Did the family suffer because your father was in the Judenrat?
Yes.
As a matter of fact, it is not only family suffer, you see, my father had to protect, let's say, himself too, myself, my brother, my sister.
They had to have a Jewish police, because they wanted to have the Jewish police to patrol the Jewish ghetto, the area, not to let the Jews out.
How can a Jew not let another Jew out?
Well, he has to make a living there.
So he looked the other way, he went away.
For this, if they found a Jew outside the ghetto, they used to take the police to jail.
And I was appointed to be part of the sanitation committee.
We used to go in, because the part that people lived in was so crowded that it came typhus with bugs and lice and so on.
And the people did not have any means to wash themselves.
So we used to-- the Judenrat use to bring soap.
We used to go into the street with the soap for the people, take them once a week or once in two weeks, because as was possible, to the bath, to the mikvah.
That's the only ritual bath-- only bath that could be.
There was no lake that they could bathe and they then, of course, they didn't want to go, because they were cold to go out of the house.
In the house, they were sticking to each other and get warm.
But we had to be the bad ones to take them there.
This was the repercussion, let's say like, we are bad to them.
But what we meant was good for all.
Otherwise they would-- the Germans would come, throw out, and put the house on fire, because the town had a lot of typhus.
As a matter of fact, I myself was sick of typhus and so was my brother, but we survived.
My father went until they killed him.
It was Yom Kippur, 1942.
Warsaw was already being deported on and off.
The next town-- the county town-- Minsk Mazowiecki, was deported a week before.
And we were expected.
The Germans came and they said, we need-- and the day before, or was it on Yom Kippur '42 or the day before?
They need people to go to the camps.
There were labor camps in the area.
The labor camps consisted of digging ditches, draining, because there was a lot of water.
And they were making roads, they should not-- and they need all people should come, Yom Kippur, to go to work.
A lot of them went away before, and all of them run away.
So when they came on Yom Kippur through it, everybody was hiding, even many of the Judenrat were hiding because they were afraid.
Who knows what's going to happen?
But my father couldn't hide.
He was staying with them the whole day and showing them.
Look, there are no young people.
They went already to the camps.
They already working there.
So they started to take out the older people, 50, 60 and 50 years old at that time was a man today like 80, with the undernourished and so on and beat them up and so on.
So I was going with them to help, not to bring so much suffering.
And when the day passed, town was almost like a ghost town.
They-- it was already dark, and they called from the precinct, the Germans called from the precinct, for my father to come to them.
And they put him into jail, incarcerated.
About an hour, we were waiting for him to come back.
He didn't.
So some other members of Judenrat that were there went to see what he's doing.
And they told them to tell us we should come to say goodbye, because they're taking him to Warsaw.
They're taking my father.
They're sending him to Warsaw.
So my brother and I went to see my father in jail.
We came over and shouted in the window, Father, we're here.
Will you come to see us?
He didn't show up and then we heard a shot that was in the square right nearby.
And there was a shot-- two shots.
There was another-- another man was in jail for something else.
They took out my father and the other man.
They shot them both.
We came over, and we saw the shots and this was the time when-- And how long did you remain in--
Well, we remained there-- the funeral was on a Sunday.
They did not allow, but a lot of-- the Germans left already.
But the funeral, everybody went.
We had to bury him in the cemetery.
And a lot of people went from different places around to the funeral.
And when we came back, I think it was a Tuesday.
The funeral was on Monday, if I'm not mistaken.
Yes, the funeral was a Monday, because the following day, we were sitting Shivah.
And we heard that the Germans are going to come soon to send us out to labor camps.
That's what they called them, because although Treblinka was not far from us, we didn't know it.
We didn't know that Treblinka existed.
But it was so quietly, and everybody was afraid to talk about it.
And a few days later, they took us out all on the square in the market to send us to Treblinka with wagons to the train.
The train was 5 kilometers from us, Mrozy.
I took some pails to bring in water from the river to help out the people were sitting in the market.
A few times I went.
Then I saw it was getting closer to evening.
They're going start moving out.
I did not come back.
I had a friend with me.
We both hid in a stall, looking out and see what's happening.
A stall, horse.
Oh, a stall, a horse stall.
Stall, yeah.
We were there looking out and see what happened.
And then we noticed that one of the Germans had a Pole on his neck and taking him to this, putting him against the wall-- we were inside-- and shot him, because he had some things.
He robbed a house, and they wanted to-- they weren't allowed to.
We were survived.
I mean, it came evening.
We ran away to one of the labor camps that were still there.
And then I've been there.
I found out that my brother also ran away to another camp, to a camp 33 kilometers, Siedlce, that they worked on the road, a German company called the Wolfer and Gobel.
Wolfer and Gobel.
Wolfer.
W-O-L-F-E-R.
And Gobel.
Gobel is G-O- umlaut G-O with two dots B-E-L.
Wolfer and Gobel.
They were engineers or a road company.
They were fixing the roads, making roads.
And they used us Jews as workers.
That's all the time, the Jews worked during the occupation.
Occasionally, they allowed the Jews to work at the sheep factory, made the coats-- sheep coat factory.
Made them to work, because they were using this for them.
And all the others had to go to work on the roads or similar hard work.
No, not Wolfer and Gobel.
They were not organized by the Germans.
No.
Wolfer and Gobel used to take people to work and they used to pay.
But they paid so much money that people didn't want to go to work.
So the-- and the Judenrat was forced to give the people-- to send the people to work.
So in this case, the Judenrat was also the bad guy, because they told them to go to work.

To make it easier, the Judenrat used to give extra five zlotys a day, whatever Wolfer and Gobel paid, they used to get five zlotys a day and a pound of bread-- a half a kilo bread-- additional, so that they-- because the pay was so much that they didn't want to go.
Quote, unquote, 'so much.' So those that didn't want to go.
But they were giving them the bread.
So they left the bread for the family, and they went, took a piece of bread, and they got some soup at the work and that's how they survived.
The labor camps was for-- not--
Not forced, not concentration camp.
They took you to camp.
They gave you in the morning like a soup and a piece of bread, then at night or whatever there, and we used to dig ditches.
Because irrigation, to have-- there was a railroad not far away.
So there used to be in the fall and the rainy time used to be mushy.
And the rails used to go down.
They couldn't work.
So they had to drain the area from the water so the rails should stay in place.
I was hidden overnight.
And I went to a labor camp.
Called Mienia.
M-I-E-N-I-A.
And that was a labor camp.
That was a labor camp.
It wasn't forced, because at that time, we forced ourselves to go, they should let us in.
There were Germans and Poles, they used to be.
And many times they used to take people to lead the Jews.
They appointed like for arbiter or what you call it?
A foreman.
You see, because they couldn't have so many.
Many of the Jews wanted to be foreman, not so much they wanted not to work, but at least take away the meanness of the Poles and the Germans.
Because when they came over to say, why didn't you do this, they didn't say, why you didn't do it, they just hit them.
In Mienia?
In Mienia, I was there since, let's say, September maybe 25th of '42 till about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till about the end of November-- till about the end of November.
Because in December, the Germans let the camps go, and they re-established Kaluszyn as another ghetto.
They brought all the people there were.
Instead of-- they pulled them from each camp, Treblinka, or wherever they were sending them, they took all the Jews into one place and now we got them all at once.
Back to the ghetto, December 1.
They established a ghetto that all the Jews that live in the area had to be in that ghetto.
If they'll be found outside, they'll be shot.
So many of them were hiding and they still came back.
And we were there nine days.
We being, we Jews, my brother and I.
My mother was sent in September to Treblinka.
I never saw my mother and my sister again.
My brother ran away one area, one place separately, and I ran to Mienia, to another place.
And we both came back to Kaluszyn on December 9-- on December 1.
There was a room, maybe 15, 20 people.
There were apartments-- the apartment, two, three rooms.
The two, three rooms consisted of a kitchen and a bedroom.
There were no such thing as a lot of room.
Anybody that had a place to lay down on the floor.
So that's how we lived.
No, there was no work.
There was nothing.
We were trying to get-- where's some money?
We used buy the bread from the Polish bakers and so on.
But we were only there nine days.
But being in the ghetto, one of the police-- of the German police and Polish police-- found out that my brother and I are alive, that we are there.
Being that they knew that we always used to get things, but they know that-- they called us the night before, on December 8, in the evening, they told some where are the [POLISH]?
So my brother and I went over to see them.
And my brother said, no, you know something, let's not go both together.
I'll go by myself, in case, who knows what can happen?
When he went there, they gave him an order for-- we need-- I will need something for his wife.
The other one needs a pair of earrings.
And a big order of a big business.
So my brother says, look, we just came in.
We'll have to look around.
We'll see what happens.
And we found out in the morning, we got up about 6:00, we hear already shooting.
And there we were with the Germans and the Polish police and the Lithuanians and Latvians and Ukrainians were getting to deport us again.
So I ran away from wherever I was with a friend of mine that's in the picture-- in the picture.
And we wanted to get out of town.
There was a policeman that used to practically live at our house and eat, stretched his gun and shooting at us.
He didn't catch me, but he caught my friend, died right in front of me.
And I ran back to a house where I knew that there is a basement.
And I ran into the basement, stayed there the whole day.
And about 3:00, 4:00 in the morning, I walked out.
And I walked to Minsk Mazowiecki, 16 kilometers, 10 miles.
Because I knew that there are still Jews working on the railroad.
And I was there for about 10 days until I found a man from the Polish underground that used to help out Jews get new identities, Polish passports.
And that time, another man from my town, we met together and said, lets get an apartment.
Also was getting two passports and that meant we'll get us the place, and we'll go to Warsaw to stay there, what happened.
And we did that.
That was in December, more or less about December 15-- December 15 in 1942.
And we were living in an apartment of a woman that her husband was a military-- an officer, Polish officer.
Whether this officer was dead already or was captured, we don't know exactly.
But he wasn't there.
They weren't sure whether he was alive or not.
And we also got news that the Jews in Warsaw ghetto are still there and they going outside to do clean up the broken up buildings, the broken up houses.
And this is where they work.
And they used to get-- take out some merchandise to sell to Poles.
And sort of-- and we met [POLISH] from our town.
And we met him and he said, it's OK to come in.
And they'll know a day or two before, and being that we'll have a place outside, we'll just go out to work and we'll never come back to work.
We'll stay in the place.
That was our intention.
That was our plan.
Coming into Warsaw ghetto, my friend had a sister and brother-in-law and two nephews.
He went to live with them, and I found out that I had cousins live in Warsaw, right in the same street.
A block away in the ghetto.
So I went to the cousins.
They were happy to see me, and I was happy with them.
And we were there since before New Year's till the December 18.
On December 18-- I'm sorry, January.
January 18, 1943.
On January 18, we heard a lot of commotions.
Of course, we had-- my cousins had prepared themselves in case anything happens, we'll be in the bunkers underneath the buildings.
And the entrance and the exit was so masqueraded, that was not easy.
You are tired.
Are you tired?
It was not easy to find out.
And they knew that we were there because they saw from the smoke or something that people are there.
Not be able to get us out.
There are gates to come into a building.
And they threw a bomb in on the floor, on the ground under the gate and that came in-- from the bomb, we got gassed.
We had entrance to the sewers, on a separate area entered the sewers, so that we can run away outside or inside.
So my cousins and I went in through the sewers and to look for a place to get.
But before that, my brother--

Before that, my brother and some of our friends went in across the street to see what the other ones are doing, because there were other people lived across the street, to find what the other ones are doing.
And they never came back.
So then they got us out, and we started to look where they are, and we went into another bunker.
We found some people there.
It was a bakery.
And we hid under the-- under the flour.
And then about a day later, they came to see us and caught us.
And of course, they found some ammunition, a few guns, and they asked who is that?
I said, I am not here.
I just came in tonight.
I have not belong here.
And they let us out.
We were so much gassed that we couldn't walk, my cousin especially, the older cousin, was gassed.
I had to carry him.
And we came in to the umschlagplatz, to the market where the railroad leaves.
A German came over, took his arms and said, you don't have to drag him.
He is not going to go far, took him away and they shot him in front of  me.
